learning_outcomes.description: Apply research principles and methods to understand scientific literature in clinical practice and health-related professions.
Evaluate analytical techniques used in quantitative and qualitative research in clinical settings.
Discuss ethical standards, appreciate cultural competence, and acknowledge Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives in the research context.
Apply research principles and methods to evaluate the effectiveness of intervention options to address clinical challenges.
Demonstrate cognitive skills to review, analyse and synthesise published research to identify and provide solutions to complex clinical problems.
Critically evaluate literature and generate clinical recommendations for complex clinical problems.
Demonstrate effective communication skills to present a clear and coherent exposition of knowledge and ideas to health-related professionals.

assessments.assessment_title: Assignment 1 - Critical Appraisal of Quantitative Literature - Individual Written (Modules 1-4)
assessments.task_description:
This assignment aims to develop students’ ability to find, read, and critically analyse verifiable literature on a clinically relevant topic, with emphasis on systematic reviews and intervention studies. Students will critically evaluate the evidence by examining strengths, limitations, and clinical applicability, and synthesise findings to inform practice. Using a provided clinical scenario and question, students will analyse a given systematic review and identify three additional intervention studies. Students will integrate and evaluate the evidence to generate justified, evidence-based recommendations to guide colleagues within your discipline. The assignment ends with a concise critical appraisal report that outlines a recommended therapy while acknowledging potential limitations and contextual challenges in applying the evidence.

  • Students will receive a relevant systematic review and are required to identify three additional relevant intervention studies.
  • Maximum word limit of 2200 words (excluding title/reference pages)
  • Your assignment should include a maximum of 10 references in total: the provided systematic review, three relevant intervention studies selected by you, and up to six additional supporting references.


Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. 

Please see the Assignment Task Sheet [A1] for details.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The assignment should be uploaded electronically using the correct Turnitin portal through the course Blackboard site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Assignment 2 - Critical Appraisal of Qualitative Literature - Group Presentation (Video) (Module 5)
assessments.task_description:
This assessment is designed to develop students’ ability to critically engage with qualitative research and apply it meaningfully within a clinical context. Through a recorded journal club presentation, students will demonstrate their capacity to analyse qualitative research, evaluate the trustworthiness and relevance of findings, and interpret their implications for practice. They will also consider ethical, cultural, and/or Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives, while communicating their insights clearly and professionally to a defined target audience. Working collaboratively, students will analyse complex information and apply evidence to inform clinical reasoning and decision-making within a real-world context.

  • In groups of four, students will create an 8–10 mins recorded journal club presentation based on a provided qualitative research article.
  • Maximum length of the group presentation is 10 mins - Marker will stop watching after 10 mins so any content over this limit will not count towards your mark for the assessment task.
  • Your presentation should include the assigned qualitative article, along with up to 4 additional sources to support your presentation (a maximum of 5 references in total)


While a group mark is typically awarded for the group video presentation, individual marks may be adjusted to reflect each student’s demonstrated contribution, including participation in the recorded presentation.

When a student is required to complete an oral or practical assessment item worth 20% or more, the performance will be recorded and retained per Appendix 1. (Assessment Procedure (95)).

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. 

Please see the Assignment Task Sheet [A2] for details.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The assignment should be uploaded electronically using the correct Turnitin portal through the course Blackboard site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Essay/ Critique, Quiz
assessments.task_description:
Six short quizzes will be conducted during scheduled tutorial sessions across the semester. These are designed to assess your overall understanding of key concepts in quantitative and qualitative methods, including systematic reviews, covered across the modules. Each quiz will consist of multiple-choice questions and will be completed under closed-book conditions.

Quizzes must be completed in person during tutorials, and any missed quiz will receive a mark of zero. To provide some flexibility, your final quiz grade will be calculated based on your best four results out of the six quizzes.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person during tutorials. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Quizzes must be completed in person during tutorials.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 10(27 Jul - 11 Oct)
learning_activities.topic:
Online Lectures via Zoom

Six 1-hour online lectures are delivered across different weeks. Four lectures take place in Week 1 to introduce the course and assessment tasks and to support learning in evaluating quantitative literature. One lecture in Week 6 focuses on synthesising findings from quantitative research, and an additional lecture in Week 10 addresses the evaluation of qualitative literature.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 2 To Week 12(03 Aug - 25 Oct)
learning_activities.topic:
Modules 1-5 - Self-paced (online learning)

These self-paced online modules run weekly from Weeks 2 to 12 and align with each tutorial session. You should complete them independently, ideally before attending the corresponding tutorial.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
learning_activities.topic_description: Modules 1-5 - Self-paced (online learning) These self-paced online modules run weekly from Weeks 2 to 12 and align with each tutorial session. You should complete them independently, ideally before attending the corresponding tutorial.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 3 To Week 12(10 Aug - 25 Oct)
learning_activities.topic:
Tutorials (in-person)

The discipline-specific tutorials (2 hours each) are held in person (on-campus) in Weeks 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, and 12. Delivered in a journal club format, these sessions focus on critically appraising practice-relevant papers using CASP checklists, with guided, hands-on activities to apply lecture concepts and strengthen understanding of research quality and relevance. They also provide targeted support for assignment development, including feedback and clarification of expectations. In-person quizzes are conducted during these tutorials.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L07
